title: Meta Stock at $625.50: Growth Bet on AI Infrastructure—But at What Cost?
description: Meta is trading at $625.50 with strong analyst support ($838–$863 price targets) and a 27.6x P/E ratio. The company is betting aggressively on AI—committing $115–$135B in 2026 capex alone—but faces massive structural risks: $80B in Reality Labs losses, planned 20% workforce cuts, and no clear monetization path for its AI spending.
verdict:
**Score: 6/10**

Meta is a legitimately profitable advertising company with $200.97B in annual revenue ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) and analyst consensus of $838–$863 price targets ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/meta/forecast/)), implying 34–38% upside from $625.50. The case rests on AI-driven ad improvements paying off. **What's good:** Strong profitability, $23.43B in free cash flow ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)), and a wide economic moat with 4-star Morningstar rating ([Morningstar](https://global.morningstar.com/en-nd/stocks/after-earnings-is-meta-stock-buy-sell-or-fairly-valued-4)). Forward P/E of 21.55x ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) is reasonable for a 20%+ growth company. Massive analyst buy-side support. **What's concerning:** $115–$135B in 2026 capex ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/))—a 73% increase—with no direct monetization path yet. $80B in accumulated Reality Labs losses ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/NASDAQ-META/)) with zero near-term profitability. Planned 20% workforce cuts ([Investing.com](https://www.investing.com/analysis/meta-stock-rising-on-massive-layoff-plans-200676702)) suggest management sees slower growth ahead, contradicting the capex thesis. **Bottom line:** This isn't "undervalued"—it's "fairly priced if capex works." Wait for Q1 2026 earnings (Apr 29, [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) to see if ad revenue is actually accelerating before committing real money. If you're already a believer in AI-driven ad targeting, this is reasonable at current levels. If you want conviction, wait for proof.

content.headline: Meta Stock at $625.50: Growth Bet on AI Infrastructure—But at What Cost?
content.summary: Meta is trading at $625.50 with strong analyst support ($838–$863 price targets) and a 27.6x P/E ratio. The company is betting aggressively on AI—committing $115–$135B in 2026 capex alone—but faces massive structural risks: $80B in Reality Labs losses, planned 20% workforce cuts, and no clear monetization path for its AI spending.
content.foolClaim: Meta is a solid growth buy backed by analyst consensus and improving profitability metrics, with massive AI infrastructure investments positioning it for long-term earnings expansion.

candidates.reasonConsidered: Meta trades below analyst price targets ($625.50 vs. $838–$863 consensus ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/meta/forecast/), [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/))), has delivered 66% returns in 2024 ([FinanceCharts](https://www.financecharts.com/stocks/META/performance/total-return)), and generates $200.97B in annual revenue with a 30% profit margin ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)). Morningstar assigns an $850 fair value and wide economic moat ([Morningstar](https://global.morningstar.com/en-nd/stocks/after-earnings-is-meta-stock-buy-sell-or-fairly-valued-4)).
candidates.reasonEliminated: Capital intensity is extreme: $115–$135B 2026 capex (73% YoY increase) with no direct monetization vehicle ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)). Reality Labs has burned $80B cumulatively with no profitability path ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/NASDAQ-META/)). Planned 20% workforce cuts signal margin pressure ahead ([Investing.com](https://www.investing.com/analysis/meta-stock-rising-on-massive-layoff-plans-200676702)). Forward P/E of 21.55x still assumes aggressive earnings growth that may not materialize if capex doesn't translate to ad revenue gains.

content.analysis:
## What They Got Right

- **Analyst consensus is genuinely bullish.** 61 of 67 analysts rate META as Buy ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/meta/forecast/)), with average price targets of $838.50–$863.20 ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/meta/forecast/), [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/))—implying 34–38% upside from $625.50. This isn't noise; it reflects institutional belief that the company's AI bets will pay off.

- **The business is genuinely profitable.** $200.97B in trailing revenue ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) with 30% profit margins ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) and $23.43B in levered free cash flow ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) means Meta generates real cash—not vaporware.

- **Recent performance has been strong.** 2024 delivered +66% returns ([FinanceCharts](https://www.financecharts.com/stocks/META/performance/total-return)), and the 5-year CAGR sits around 20–21% ([FinanceCharts](https://www.financecharts.com/stocks/META/performance/total-return)), placing META in the top tier of large-cap tech performers.

- **Valuation is reasonable relative to growth.** The PEG ratio (P/E divided by expected earnings growth rate) is 1.05 ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/))—meaning the stock trades roughly in line with its growth expectations, not at an extreme premium.

## What They Missed

- **The capex math doesn't add up yet.** Meta is spending $115–$135B in 2026 capex ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/))—a 73% year-over-year increase—mostly on AI data centers. But here's the problem: Meta doesn't have a cloud business to monetize this compute (unlike Amazon AWS or Microsoft Azure). All that infrastructure is internal—spent to improve ad targeting. If ads don't grow proportionally, this becomes a cash sink, not an investment. The market is betting that 2026 will prove the ROI. If it doesn't, 2027 capex guidance could crater, and the stock could too.

- **Reality Labs is a $80B disaster with no exit plan.** Cumulative losses sit at ~$80B since 2020 ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/NASDAQ-META/)), and Meta continues to bleed cash on VR/AR hardware (Quest) and Ray-Ban glasses. Meta cut 1,000 Reality Labs jobs in January ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/NASDAQ-META/)), signaling the unit is not close to breakeven. This isn't a small bet—it's a massive drag on free cash flow that investors rationalize as "future optionality." But at $80B cumulative losses with no clear consumer adoption, it's starting to look like a sunk cost fallacy.

- **Layoffs mean margin pressure, not savings.** Meta plans to cut ~20% of its 78,865-person workforce ([Investing.com](https://www.investing.com/analysis/meta-stock-rising-on-massive-layoff-plans-200676702), [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/))—that's ~15,700 jobs. While this sounds like a margin-friendly move, it typically signals that management sees slower revenue growth ahead and needs to right-size costs. The market is pricing this as "prudent cost control," but it could also mean Meta's organic growth is slowing and capex isn't translating to ad revenue fast enough. Layoffs + massive capex = a company under pressure, not one in control.

- **Analyst targets assume the AI bet works—but they always do.** The $838–$863 consensus price target ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/meta/forecast/), [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) bakes in aggressive assumptions about 2026–2027 earnings growth. If capex delivers a +15% ad growth boost, great. If it delivers +3%, the forward P/E of 21.55x ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) suddenly looks expensive, and analysts will downgrade. This is not "undervalued"—it's "fairly valued if everything goes right."

## The Bottom Line

Meta is a profitable, cash-generative advertising giant betting heavily on AI to sustain growth. The analyst consensus and Morningstar's $850 fair value ([Morningstar](https://global.morningstar.com/en-nd/stocks/after-earnings-is-meta-stock-buy-sell-or-fairly-valued-4)) are credible. But the recommendation skips over the structural risks: a $115–$135B capex budget ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) with no clear monetization path, $80B in accumulated Reality Labs losses ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/NASDAQ-META/)), and 20% workforce cuts signaling slower growth ahead. This isn't a slam-dunk buy at $625.50—it's a "bet on execution." If you're bullish on AI-driven ad targeting, this is reasonable. If you want a margin of safety, wait for 2026 earnings (due Apr 29, [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) to confirm capex is delivering ROI.
risks: Capital expenditure of $115–$135B in 2026 does not yet have a proven return on investment ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)); if ad revenue growth disappoints, stock could decline sharply.

Reality Labs cumulative losses of ~$80B ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/NASDAQ-META/)) with no path to profitability represent a permanent capital drain and drag on overall returns.

Planned 20% workforce cuts (~15,700 jobs) ([Investing.com](https://www.investing.com/analysis/meta-stock-rising-on-massive-layoff-plans-200676702), [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) signal management expects slower growth and operational stress, contradicting the bullish analyst thesis.
Forward P/E of 21.55x ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) assumes aggressive earnings growth; any miss on 2026 capex ROI could trigger multiple compression and 20–30% drawdown.
Regulatory risk: Meta faces antitrust scrutiny globally; unfavorable outcomes could force divestitures or constrain ad pricing power.
Competition from Alphabet (GOOGL) in digital advertising and AI is intensifying; Meta's ad-targeting edge may erode as competitors improve AI models.
catalysts: Q1 2026 earnings (Apr 29, [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) will show whether capex-driven improvements are translating to higher ad revenue and margins.

Full-year 2026 capex guidance (expected mid-2026) will confirm or refute the $115–$135B estimate ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)); any downward revision could signal capex ROI is lower than expected.
Reality Labs loss trajectory: if losses continue to widen or capex increases, this could trigger investor calls for unit separation or shutdown.

AI chip partnership announcements (e.g., $100B AMD deal ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/video/amd-metas-100b-ai-deal-154010623.html))) could signal cost savings vs. Nvidia, improving capex efficiency.
Regulatory outcomes on antitrust in U.S., EU, or U.K. could impose restrictions on ad targeting or require business restructuring.
content.finalVerdict:
**Score: 6/10**

Meta is a legitimately profitable advertising company with $200.97B in annual revenue ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) and analyst consensus of $838–$863 price targets ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/meta/forecast/)), implying 34–38% upside from $625.50. The case rests on AI-driven ad improvements paying off. **What's good:** Strong profitability, $23.43B in free cash flow ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)), and a wide economic moat with 4-star Morningstar rating ([Morningstar](https://global.morningstar.com/en-nd/stocks/after-earnings-is-meta-stock-buy-sell-or-fairly-valued-4)). Forward P/E of 21.55x ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) is reasonable for a 20%+ growth company. Massive analyst buy-side support. **What's concerning:** $115–$135B in 2026 capex ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/))—a 73% increase—with no direct monetization path yet. $80B in accumulated Reality Labs losses ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/NASDAQ-META/)) with zero near-term profitability. Planned 20% workforce cuts ([Investing.com](https://www.investing.com/analysis/meta-stock-rising-on-massive-layoff-plans-200676702)) suggest management sees slower growth ahead, contradicting the capex thesis. **Bottom line:** This isn't "undervalued"—it's "fairly priced if capex works." Wait for Q1 2026 earnings (Apr 29, [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)) to see if ad revenue is actually accelerating before committing real money. If you're already a believer in AI-driven ad targeting, this is reasonable at current levels. If you want conviction, wait for proof.
